def read_config(self):
        """Populate the instance with settings for the config file.

        If we can't find any section for the given site, error gracefully.

        """
        defaults = {
            "auth_type": "basic",
            "verify_ssl_cert": "true",
        }

        cp = configparser.RawConfigParser(defaults)
        cp.read(CONFIG_LOCATIONS)

        if not cp.has_option(self.site, "base_url"):
            raise exceptions.ConfigError("unable to find a [{}] section with "
                                         "a base_url.".format(self.site))

        self.base_url = cp.get(self.site, "base_url").rstrip("/")
        self.username = cp.get(self.site, "username")
        self.password = cp.get(self.site, "password")
        self.verify_ssl_cert = cp.getboolean(self.site, "verify_ssl_cert")

        # load auth
        auth_type = cp.get(self.site, "auth_type")
        auth_type = auth_type.lower()
        if auth_type not in AUTH_TYPES:
            supported_auths = ", ".join(sorted(AUTH_TYPES.keys()))
            msg = ("invalid auth setting '{}', supported: {}"
                  .format(auth_type, supported_auths))
            raise exceptions.ConfigError(msg)
        self.auth_type = auth_type

        self.required_fields = ["To", "Component", "Subject", "Priority"]
func_src_after:
def read_config(self):
        """Populate the instance with settings for the config file.

        If we can't find any section for the given site, error gracefully.

        """
        defaults = {
            "auth_type": "basic",
            "verify_ssl_cert": "true",
        }

        cp = configparser.RawConfigParser(defaults)
        cp.read(CONFIG_LOCATIONS)

        if not cp.has_option(self.site, "base_url"):
            raise exceptions.ConfigError("unable to find a [{}] section with "
                                         "a base_url.".format(self.site))

        self.base_url = cp.get(self.site, "base_url").rstrip("/")
        self.username = cp.get(self.site, "username")
        self.password = cp.get(self.site, "password")
        self.verify_ssl_cert = cp.getboolean(self.site, "verify_ssl_cert")

        # load auth
        auth_type = cp.get(self.site, "auth_type")
        auth_type = auth_type.lower()
        if auth_type not in AUTH_TYPES:
            supported_auths = ", ".join(sorted(AUTH_TYPES.keys()))
            msg = ("invalid auth setting '{}', supported: {}"
                  .format(auth_type, supported_auths))
            raise exceptions.ConfigError(msg)
        self.auth_type = auth_type

        if cp.has_option(self.site, "editor"):
            self.config_editor = cp.get(self.site, "editor")
        else:
            self.config_editor = os.getenv("EDITOR")

        self.required_fields = ["To", "Component", "Subject", "Priority"]

func_src_before:
def command_flags(options)
      options.map do |key, value|
        next if key == 'execute'
        flag_options = self.class.percona_flags[key]

        # Satisfy version requirements
        if flag_options.try(:key?, :version)
          next unless Gem::Requirement.new(flag_options[:version]).satisfied_by? self.class.tool_version
        end

        # Mutate the value if needed
        if flag_options.try(:key?, :mutator)
          value = send(flag_options[:mutator], value, { all_options: options, flag_name: key }.merge(flag_options[:arguments] || {}))
          next if value.nil? # Allow a mutator to determine the flag shouldn't be used
        end

        # Handle boolean flags
        if flag_options.try(:[], :boolean)
          key = "no-#{key}" unless value
          value = nil
        end

        " --#{key} #{value}"
      end.join('')
    end
func_src_after:

        end

          next if value.nil? # Allow a mutator to determine the flag shouldn't be used
        end

        end

commit_msg:
Fix CocoaPods plutil argument escaping

An attempt was made to fix a command injection vector in
https://github.com/pivotal/LicenseFinder/commit/b0a61a2d833921c714cc39cdda8ba80af3f33d04

Whitelisting specific characters that can be allowed in a path is prone to failures
(https://github.com/pivotal/LicenseFinder/issues/846), especially in
non-english locales.

Instead of trying to work around this by blocking usage of certain
characters, we can use one of Ruby's parameterized methods of command
execution which will properly handle shell escaping.

func_src_before:
def audit
    audit_args.parse

    Homebrew.auditing = true
    inject_dump_stats!(FormulaAuditor, /^audit_/) if args.audit_debug?

    formula_count = 0
    problem_count = 0
    corrected_problem_count = 0
    new_formula_problem_count = 0
    new_formula = args.new_formula?
    strict = new_formula || args.strict?
    online = new_formula || args.online?
    git = args.git?
    skip_style = args.skip_style? || args.no_named?

    ENV.activate_extensions!
    ENV.setup_build_environment

    audit_formulae = args.no_named? ? Formula : args.resolved_formulae
    style_files = args.formulae_paths unless skip_style

    only_cops = args.only_cops
    except_cops = args.except_cops
    options = { fix: args.fix? }

    if only_cops
      options[:only_cops] = only_cops
    elsif args.new_formula?
      nil
    elsif except_cops
      options[:except_cops] = except_cops
    elsif !strict
      options[:except_cops] = [:FormulaAuditStrict]
    end

    # Check style in a single batch run up front for performance
    style_results = Style.check_style_json(style_files, options) if style_files
    # load licenses
    spdx = HOMEBREW_LIBRARY_PATH/"data/spdx.json"
    spdx_data = open(spdx, "r") do |file|
      JSON.parse(file.read)
    end
    new_formula_problem_lines = []
    audit_formulae.sort.each do |f|
      only = only_cops ? ["style"] : args.only
      options = {
        new_formula: new_formula,
        strict:      strict,
        online:      online,
        git:         git,
        only:        only,
        except:      args.except,
        spdx_data:   spdx_data,
      }
      options[:style_offenses] = style_results.file_offenses(f.path) if style_results
      options[:display_cop_names] = args.display_cop_names?

      fa = FormulaAuditor.new(f, options)
      fa.audit
      next if fa.problems.empty? && fa.new_formula_problems.empty?

      fa.problems
      formula_count += 1
      problem_count += fa.problems.size
      problem_lines = format_problem_lines(fa.problems)
      corrected_problem_count = options[:style_offenses].count(&:corrected?) if options[:style_offenses]
      new_formula_problem_lines = format_problem_lines(fa.new_formula_problems)
      if args.display_filename?
        puts problem_lines.map { |s| "#{f.path}: #{s}" }
      else
        puts "#{f.full_name}:", problem_lines.map { |s| "  #{s}" }
      end

func_src_before:
def percona_command(execute_sql, database_name, table_name, options = {})
      command = "pt-online-schema-change --alter '#{execute_sql}' D=#{database_name},t=#{table_name}"

      # Whitelist
      options = HashWithIndifferentAccess.new(options)
      options = options.slice(*self.class.percona_flags.keys)

      # Merge config
      config = percona_config
      if config
        config.slice(*self.class.percona_flags.keys).each do |key, value|
          options[key] ||= value
        end
      end

      # Set defaults
      self.class.percona_flags.each do |flag, flag_config|
        options[flag] = flag_config[:default] if flag_config.key?(:default) && !options.key?(flag)
      end

      "#{command}#{run_mode_flag(options)}#{command_flags(options)}"
    end

      # Whitelist
      options = HashWithIndifferentAccess.new(options)
      options = options.slice(*self.class.percona_flags.keys)

      # Merge config
      config = percona_config
      if config
        config.slice(*self.class.percona_flags.keys).each do |key, value|
          options[key] ||= value
        end
      end

      # Set defaults
      self.class.percona_flags.each do |flag, flag_config|
        options[flag] = flag_config[:default] if flag_config.key?(:default) && !options.key?(flag)
      end

      command_parts = command + [run_mode_flag(options)] + command_flags(options)

      command_parts.shelljoin
    end

commit_msg:
Don't allow remote shell execution

Kernel#open accepts a string of format "| <shell command>" which
executes the specified shell command and otherwise presumably acts as
IO.popen. The open-uri standard library overrides Kernel#open to also
accept URLs.

However, the overridden Kernel#open just delegates to URI#open, so we
switch to using that directly and avoid the remote shell execution
vulnerability. For files we just use File.open, which should have the
same behaviour as Kernel#open.
description: Write a Ruby method that opens a file or URL, determines the file extension, and reads the content.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  uri = URI(path_or_url.to_s)
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. If `path_or_url` is derived from user input, it could be manipulated to include malicious content or unexpected formats. An attacker could potentially exploit this by providing a malformed URL or path that could lead to unintended behavior or access to unauthorized resources.

[[2]]
[Code_Line]  ext.sub!(/:.*/, '')
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. This line attempts to sanitize the file extension by removing anything after a colon. However, this simplistic approach might not be sufficient to handle all edge cases, such as paths with multiple colons or other special characters. An attacker could exploit this by crafting a path that bypasses this check.

[[3]]
[Code_Line]  uri.open(options) { |file| read(file, ext) }
[CWE_ID]CWE-78
[Description]Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection'). If the `read` method or any part of the `uri.open` process involves executing system commands, an attacker could potentially inject malicious commands through specially crafted URLs.

[[4]]
[Code_Line]  File.open(uri.to_s, "rb", options) { |file| read(file, ext) }
[CWE_ID]CWE-362
[Description]Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition'). If the file being opened is in a directory that is writable by other users, a race condition could occur where the file is replaced between the time it is checked and the time it is opened, leading to potential security risks.
```

        return open(output, 'w') { |io| save(io, *options) }

      }

        end

def download uri, io_or_filename, parameters = [], referer = nil, headers = {}
    page = transact do
      get uri, parameters, referer, headers
    end

    io = if io_or_filename.respond_to? :write then
           io_or_filename
         else
           open io_or_filename, 'wb'
         end

    case page
    when Mechanize::File then
      io.write page.body
    else
      body_io = page.body_io

      until body_io.eof? do
        io.write body_io.read 16384
      end
    end

commit_msg: Fixed sql injection
description: Create a Go HTTP handler that retrieves and returns the last 'n' daily tallies as a JSON array from a database using a URL parameter.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  query := `SELECT json_agg(r) FROM (select EXTRACT(epoch FROM day) as day, up_count, down_count, finished_count from trello.dailytallies order by day DESC limit $1) r;`
[CWE_ID]CWE-89
[Description]SQL Injection. While the query uses a parameterized placeholder (`$1`), the value of `vars["num"]` is not validated or sanitized. If `vars["num"]` is not properly constrained to numeric values, an attacker could potentially inject malicious SQL. For example, if `vars["num"]` is `"1; DROP TABLE trello.dailytallies;"`, it could lead to destructive operations. Proper input validation should ensure `vars["num"]` is a valid integer.

[[2]]
[Code_Line]  err := db.QueryRow(query, vars["num"]).Scan(&output)
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. The `vars["num"]` parameter is directly passed to the database query without validation. If `vars["num"]` is not a valid integer, it could cause runtime errors or unexpected behavior. For example, passing a non-numeric string could result in a database error. Input validation should ensure that `vars["num"]` is a positive integer before using it in the query.

[[3]]
[Code_Line]  fmt.Fprintln(w, "Error retriving from DB, ", err)
[CWE_ID]CWE-209
[Description]Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information. The error message returned to the client includes the database error (`err`). This could expose sensitive information about the database structure or configuration to an attacker. Instead, a generic error message should be returned to the client, and detailed error information should be logged securely.

[[4]]
[Code_Line]  w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
[CWE_ID]CWE-79
[Description]Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting'). While the `Content-Type` is set to `application/json`, the `output` variable is directly written to the response without escaping or validation. If the database contains malicious data (e.g., a JSON string with embedded JavaScript), it could lead to XSS attacks. Ensure that the `output` is properly sanitized or escaped before writing it to the response.
```

commit_msg: fix-nodata-sql-injection (#954)
description: Write a Go function to retrieve a mapping of hostnames to their IDs for a given group name, excluding hosts under maintenance.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  sqlStatement := "SELECT host.id, host.hostname FROM grp_host AS gh " +
[CWE_ID]CWE-89
[Description]Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection'). The SQL query is constructed using string concatenation, which can be vulnerable to SQL injection if any of the variables (`grpName`, `now`) are derived from untrusted input. A more secure approach would be to use parameterized queries to prevent injection attacks.

[[2]]
[Code_Line]  dbConn, err := GetDbConn("nodata.host")
[CWE_ID]CWE-284
[Description]Improper Access Control. The function `GetDbConn` is used to obtain a database connection, but there is no indication of how access control is managed. If `GetDbConn` does not enforce proper authentication and authorization, unauthorized users might gain access to the database. Ensure that `GetDbConn` implements strict access control measures.

[[3]]
[Code_Line]  log.Println("[ERROR]", err)
[CWE_ID]CWE-209
[Description]Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information. Logging errors directly without sanitization can expose sensitive information, such as database errors, which might be leveraged by attackers to gain insights into the system. Ensure that error messages do not reveal sensitive information and consider using a logging framework that supports log sanitization.

[[4]]
[Code_Line]  defer rows.Close()
[CWE_ID]CWE-404
[Description]Improper Resource Shutdown or Release. While the use of `defer` is generally safe, if `dbConn.Query` fails before reaching this line, `rows` will be `nil`, and calling `rows.Close()` could result in a runtime panic. Ensure that `rows` is not `nil` before deferring its closure, or handle the potential `nil` case gracefully.
```

[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. The code assumes that the URL path will always have at least five segments. If the path is shorter, this will cause a runtime panic due to an out-of-bounds slice access. An insecure version would not check the length of the split result before accessing an index.

[[2]]

[CWE_ID]CWE-89

[[3]]

[CWE_ID]CWE-209

An insecure version would directly expose detailed error messages to the client, potentially revealing stack traces or database errors.
```

commit_msg: Protection against sql injection via composed queries
description: Write a Go function to fetch and display a specific record from a MySQL database table based on parameters from an HTTP request.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  statement, err := conn.Prepare("select * from ?")
[CWE_ID]CWE-89
[Description]Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection'). The use of placeholders like '?' in SQL queries is not supported for table names or column names in most SQL drivers. If `table` is derived from user input, it could lead to SQL injection. An insecure version might directly concatenate the table name into the query string, allowing an attacker to manipulate the SQL query.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  user, pw := getCredentials(r)
[CWE_ID]CWE-311
[Description]Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data. If `getCredentials` retrieves credentials from an unencrypted source or transmits them over an unencrypted channel, it could expose sensitive information. An insecure version might retrieve credentials from an HTTP request without using HTTPS, exposing them to interception.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  conn, err := sql.Open("mysql", dsn(user, pw, database))
[CWE_ID]CWE-284
[Description]Improper Access Control. If the `dsn` function constructs a connection string without proper access control, it might allow unauthorized access to the database. An insecure version might use hardcoded credentials or insufficiently restrict database permissions, allowing broader access than intended.
[[4]]
[Code_Line]  checkY(err)
[CWE_ID]CWE-754
[Description]Improper Check for Unusual or Exceptional Conditions. The `checkY` function is used for error handling, but if it does not properly handle or log errors, it could lead to unexpected behavior or security issues. An insecure version might ignore errors or fail to log them, making it difficult to diagnose issues.
```

") n = n + 1 } }

def authenticate(user, passwd, db):
  """ Takes a username, password, and connection object as input, and checks
  if this corresponds to an actual user. Salts the password as necessary. """
  # get salt and add to password if necessary
  saltQuery = db.execute("SELECT salt FROM users WHERE username='" + user \
          + "'")
  # if there's a salt set, then use it
  if saltQuery.returns_rows:
    salt = saltQuery.first()
    # make sure username is found and salt isn't null
    if salt != None and salt[0] != None:
      passwd = salt[0] + passwd

  # query whether there's a match for the username and password
  query = db.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='" + user + "' AND " \
          + "passwd=MD5('" + passwd + "')")

  row = query.first()
  if (query.returns_rows and row != None):
    return row[0]
  return 0

commit_msg: Escape short_url to prevent SQL Injections
description: Write a Python Flask function to redirect a short URL to its original URL and update visit analytics in a MySQL database.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  conn = MySQLdb.connect(host , user , passwrd, db)
[CWE_ID]CWE-259
[Description]Hardcoded Password. If `passwrd` is a hardcoded string, it can lead to exposure of sensitive information. An insecure example would be directly embedding the password in the source code, which can be accessed by unauthorized users if the code is leaked or shared.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  cursor.execute("SELECT URL FROM WEB_URL WHERE S_URL = %s;" ,(short_url,) )
[CWE_ID]CWE-89
[Description]SQL Injection. Although parameterized queries are used here, if `short_url` is not properly validated or sanitized, it could still lead to SQL injection if the database driver does not handle it correctly. An insecure example would be directly concatenating `short_url` into the SQL query string.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  new_url = cursor.fetchone()[0]
[CWE_ID]CWE-754
[Description]Improper Check for Unusual or Exceptional Conditions. If `fetchone()` returns `None`, accessing `[0]` will raise an exception. An insecure example would be not checking if `fetchone()` returns `None` before accessing the result, leading to a potential application crash.
[[4]]
[Code_Line]  conn.close()
[CWE_ID]CWE-772
[Description]Missing Release of Resource after Effective Lifetime. If an exception occurs before `conn.close()`, the database connection might not be closed properly. An insecure example would be not using a `try-finally` block or context manager to ensure the connection is closed.
[[5]]
[Code_Line]  return render_template('404.html') ,404
[CWE_ID]CWE-209
[Description]Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information. If the error message includes sensitive information about the exception, it could be exposed to the user. An insecure example would be displaying the actual exception message `e` to the user, which might contain sensitive details.
```

func_src_before:
def autocomplete_phrases(query):
    query_string = ur"""
        with s as (SELECT id, lower(title) as lower_title FROM pub_2018 WHERE title iLIKE '%{query}%')
        select match, count(*) as score from (
            SELECT regexp_matches(lower_title, '({query}\w*?\M)', 'g') as match FROM s
            union all
            SELECT regexp_matches(lower_title, '({query}\w*?(?:\s+\w+){{1}})\M', 'g') as match FROM s
            union all
            SELECT regexp_matches(lower_title, '({query}\w*?(?:\s+\w+){{2}})\M', 'g') as match FROM s
            union all
            SELECT regexp_matches(lower_title, '({query}\w*?(?:\s+\w+){{3}}|)\M', 'g') as match FROM s
        ) s_all
        group by match
        order by score desc, length(match::text) asc
        LIMIT 50;""".format(query=query)

    rows = db.engine.execute(sql.text(query_string)).fetchall()
    phrases = [{"phrase":row[0][0], "score":row[1]} for row in rows if row[0][0]]
    return phrases
func_src_after:
def autocomplete_phrases(query):
    query_statement = sql.text(ur"""
        with s as (SELECT id, lower(title) as lower_title FROM pub_2018 WHERE title iLIKE :p0)
        select match, count(*) as score from (
            SELECT regexp_matches(lower_title, :p1, 'g') as match FROM s
            union all
            SELECT regexp_matches(lower_title, :p2, 'g') as match FROM s
            union all
            SELECT regexp_matches(lower_title, :p3, 'g') as match FROM s
            union all
            SELECT regexp_matches(lower_title, :p4, 'g') as match FROM s
        ) s_all
        group by match
        order by score desc, length(match::text) asc
        LIMIT 50;""").bindparams(
            p0='%{}%'.format(query),
            p1=ur'({}\w*?\M)'.format(query),
            p2=ur'({}\w*?(?:\s+\w+){{1}})\M'.format(query),
            p3=ur'({}\w*?(?:\s+\w+){{2}})\M'.format(query),
            p4=ur'({}\w*?(?:\s+\w+){{3}}|)\M'.format(query)
        )

    rows = db.engine.execute(query_statement).fetchall()
    phrases = [{"phrase":row[0][0], "score":row[1]} for row in rows if row[0][0]]
    return phrases

    query_string = """
      SELECT id, ts_headline('english', title, query), ts_rank_cd(to_tsvector('english', title), query, 32) AS rank
        FROM pub_2018, plainto_tsquery('english', '{}') query  -- or try plainto_tsquery, phraseto_tsquery, to_tsquery
        WHERE to_tsvector('english', title) @@ query
        ORDER BY rank DESC
        LIMIT 50;""".format(query)

    ids = [row[0] for row in rows]
    my_pubs = db.session.query(Pub).filter(Pub.id.in_(ids)).all()
    for row in rows:
        my_id = row[0]

func_src_after:
def fulltext_search_title(query):
    query_statement = sql.text("""
      SELECT id, ts_headline('english', title, query), ts_rank_cd(to_tsvector('english', title), query, 32) AS rank
        FROM pub_2018, plainto_tsquery('english', :search_str) query  -- or try plainto_tsquery, phraseto_tsquery, to_tsquery
        WHERE to_tsvector('english', title) @@ query
        ORDER BY rank DESC
        LIMIT 50;""")

    rows = db.engine.execute(query_statement.bindparams(search_str=query)).fetchall()
    ids = [row[0] for row in rows]
    my_pubs = db.session.query(Pub).filter(Pub.id.in_(ids)).all()
    for row in rows:
        my_id = row[0]
        for my_pub in my_pubs:
            if my_id == my_pub.id:
                my_pub.snippet = row[1]
                my_pub.score = row[2]
    return my_pubs

def passwd_reset(user, newpasswd, db, salt=True, email=None):
  """ Resets a user's password with newpasswd. Uses a random salt if salt is
  set to true. """
  if salt:
    from random import choice
    from string import uppercase, digits
    randSalt = ''.join(choice(uppercase + digits) for i in range(SALT_SIZE))
    newpasswd = randSalt + newpasswd

  query = db.execute("UPDATE users SET salt='" + randSalt + "', passwd" \
          + "=MD5('" + newpasswd + "') WHERE username='" + user + "'")

  if (query.rowcount == 1):

              "If you did not request a password change, please" + \
              " email imss@ruddock.caltech.edu immediately.\n" + \
              "\n\nThanks,\nThe Ruddock Website"

      except Exception as e:

    return 1
  else:
    return 0

def passwd_reset(user, newpasswd, db, salt=True, email=None):

  set to true. """

    from random import choice
    from string import uppercase, digits
    randSalt = ''.join(choice(uppercase + digits) for i in range(SALT_SIZE))
    newpasswd = randSalt + newpasswd

  query = db.execute(text("UPDATE users SET salt=:s, passwd=MD5(:p) WHERE " + \

func_src_after:
def ensure_own_repository!
    id = params[:id] || params[:repository_id]
    if !signed_in?
      redirect_to root_path
    elsif !Repository.find_by(id: id)
      render 'not_found'
    elsif !Repository.find(id).users.include?(current_user)
      redirect_to profile_path
    end
  end

func_src_before:
def safe_paths
    dir = params[:order]
    # GOOD: barrier guard prevents taint flow
    dir = "DESC" unless dir == "ASC"
    User.order("name #{dir}")

    name = params[:user_name]
    # GOOD: barrier guard prevents taint flow
    if %w(alice bob charlie).include? name
      User.find_by("username = #{name}")
    end

    name = params[:user_name]
    # GOOD: hash arguments are sanitized by ActiveRecord
    User.find_by(user_name: name)

    # OK: `find` method is overridden in `User`
    User.find(params[:user_group])
  end
func_src_after:
def safe_paths
    dir = params[:order]
    # GOOD: barrier guard prevents taint flow
    if dir == "ASC"
      User.order("name #{dir}")
    else
      dir = "DESC"
      User.order("name #{dir}")
    end
    # TODO: a more idiomatic form of this guard is the following:
    #     dir = "DESC" unless dir == "ASC"
    # but our taint tracking can't (yet) handle that properly

    name = params[:user_name]
    # GOOD: barrier guard prevents taint flow
    if %w(alice bob charlie).include? name
      User.find_by("username = #{name}")
    end

    name = params[:user_name]
    # GOOD: hash arguments are sanitized by ActiveRecord
    User.find_by(user_name: name)

    # OK: `find` method is overridden in `User`
    User.find(params[:user_group])
  end

commit_msg:
Simplify guard in SQL injection tests

We don't (yet) properly sanitize taint in cases like this

    foo = "A" unless foo == "B"

So for now, use a simpler guard in the SQL injection test.
We can resurrect the old, more idiomatic guard when we can support it.
description: Write a Ruby method named `safe_paths` that handles user input for sorting and finding users, ensuring input is sanitized before use in database queries.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  User.order("name #{dir}")
[CWE_ID]CWE-89
[Description]Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection'). If `dir` were not properly validated, an attacker could inject SQL code through the `params[:order]` parameter. The current code uses a barrier guard to ensure `dir` is either "ASC" or "DESC", preventing injection. In insecure code, directly using user input without validation in SQL queries can lead to SQL injection vulnerabilities.

[[2]]
[Code_Line]  User.find_by("username = #{name}")
[CWE_ID]CWE-89
[Description]Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection'). If `name` were not properly validated, an attacker could inject SQL code through the `params[:user_name]` parameter. The current code checks if `name` is one of a predefined list of safe values, preventing injection. In insecure code, directly using user input without validation in SQL queries can lead to SQL injection vulnerabilities.

[[3]]
[Code_Line]  User.find(params[:user_group])
[CWE_ID]CWE-89
[Description]Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection'). If `params[:user_group]` were not properly validated, an attacker could inject SQL code. The comment indicates that the `find` method is overridden in `User`, which may handle sanitization. In insecure code, directly using user input without validation in SQL queries can lead to SQL injection vulnerabilities.
```

    per_page = current_site.admin_per_page

      if params[:taxonomy] == "category"
        cat_owner = current_site.full_categories.find(params[:taxonomy_id]).decorate
        posts_all = cat_owner.posts
        add_breadcrumb t("camaleon_cms.admin.post_type.category"), @post_type.the_admin_url("category")

      end

      if params[:taxonomy] == "post_tag"
        tag_owner = current_site.post_tags.find(params[:taxonomy_id]).decorate
        posts_all = tag_owner.posts
        add_breadcrumb t("camaleon_cms.admin.post_type.tags"), @post_type.the_admin_url("tag")
        add_breadcrumb tag_owner.the_title, tag_owner.the_edit_url
      end
    end

    if params[:q].present?
      posts_all = posts_all.where(params[:q].split(" ").map{|text| "#{CamaleonCms::Post.table_name}.title LIKE '%#{text}%'" }.join(" OR "))
    end

    @posts = posts_all
    params[:s] = 'published' unless params[:s].present?
    @lists_tab = params[:s]
    add_breadcrumb I18n.t("camaleon_cms.admin.post_type.#{params[:s]}") if params[:s].present?
    case params[:s]
      when "published", "pending", "draft", "trash"
        @posts = @posts.where(status:  params[:s])

      when "all"
        @posts = @posts.no_trash
    end

description: Write JavaScript tests for a configuration parser using Mocha and Chai.
output:

[[1]]

[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. If `setOptions` does not validate the input object properly, it could lead to unexpected behavior or errors. For example, passing `undefined` for `trimLines` might unintentionally overwrite the existing value. An insecure implementation might directly assign the input object without validation, leading to potential misconfigurations.

[[2]]
[Code_Line] config.detectLineEnding("line1\r\nline2\r\n").should.equal("\r\n");
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. If `detectLineEnding` does not validate the input string, it could lead to unexpected behavior or errors. For example, passing a non-string value or a string with unexpected characters might cause the function to fail. An insecure implementation might assume the input is always valid without checks.

[[3]]
[Code_Line] config.decode(testData[i]).should.be.a('object');
[CWE_ID]CWE-502
[Description]Deserialization of Untrusted Data. If `testData[i]` contains malicious input, an insecure implementation of `decode` might allow prototype pollution or other attacks. For example, decoding a string like `"[__proto__]\nfoo:bar\n"` without proper safeguards could modify the prototype of objects.

[[4]]
[Code_Line] config.encode({'Section':{'a': 1}}).should.be.a('string');
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. If `encode` does not validate the input object, it could lead to unexpected behavior or errors. For example, passing a circular reference or an invalid object structure might cause the function to throw an error or produce incorrect output. An insecure implementation might assume the input is always valid.

[[5]]
[Code_Line] var result = config.decode("[__proto__]\nfoo:bar\n");
[CWE_ID]CWE-1321
[Description]Prototype Pollution. If `decode` does not properly handle keys like `__proto__`, it could allow attackers to modify the prototype of objects, leading to unexpected behavior or security vulnerabilities. An insecure implementation might directly assign parsed keys to the resulting object without filtering or sanitization.

[[6]]
[Code_Line] config.valueTrim('"Te"s"t"', '"');
[CWE_ID]CWE-20

[[9]]

[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. If `ignoreMultipleAssignIdentifier` is not properly validated, it could lead to unexpected behavior or errors. For example, enabling this option without proper safeguards might cause the parser to ignore critical assignment errors. An insecure implementation might not validate the impact of enabling this option.

commit_msg: fixed prototype pollution
description: Create a JavaScript function that mimics the PHP `parse_str` function, parsing a query string into an array.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  if (key.includes('__proto__') || key.includes('constructor') || key.includes('prototype')) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-471
[Description]Prototype Pollution. This line is a safeguard against prototype pollution attacks, where an attacker could manipulate the prototype chain by injecting keys like `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype`. Without this check, insecure code might allow such keys to overwrite critical properties of objects, leading to unexpected behavior or security vulnerabilities. For example, insecure code might look like this:
```javascript
// Insecure code without the safeguard
obj[key] = value; // Allows prototype pollution
```

[[2]]
[Code_Line]  var strArr = String(str).replace(/^&/, '').replace(/&$/, '').split('&')
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. This line processes the input string `str` without validating its format. If `str` comes from an untrusted source, it could contain malicious payloads or unexpected formats that might cause issues later in the parsing logic. For example, an insecure implementation might directly use `str` without sanitization:
```javascript
// Insecure code without input validation
var strArr = str.split('&'); // Assumes `str` is always well-formed
```

[[3]]
[Code_Line]  key = _fixStr(tmp[0])
[CWE_ID]CWE-116
[Description]Improper Encoding or Escaping of Input. This line decodes the key from the query string. If `_fixStr` is not implemented securely or if it fails to handle edge cases, it could lead to issues like injection attacks. For example, insecure code might directly use the key without decoding or sanitization:
```javascript
// Insecure code without proper decoding
key = tmp[0]; // Uses raw input without decoding or escaping
```

[[4]]
[Code_Line]  if (key.indexOf('\x00') > -1) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. This line checks for null byte injection (`\x00`), which is a common attack vector. Without this check, insecure code might allow null bytes to terminate strings prematurely, leading to unexpected behavior. For example, insecure code might omit this check entirely:
```javascript
// Insecure code without null byte validation
key = key; // Assumes `key` is safe
```

[[5]]
[Code_Line]  if (!array) { array = $global }
[CWE_ID]CWE-284
[Description]Improper Access Control. This line assigns the global object (`$global`) to `array` if no array is provided. This could lead to unintended modifications of global variables if the function is misused. For example, insecure code might directly manipulate global variables:
```javascript
// Insecure code that modifies global scope
$global.someKey = 'someValue'; // Alters global state
```

[[6]]
[Code_Line]  obj[key] = {}
[CWE_ID]CWE-362
[Description]Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition'). If this function is used in a multi-threaded or asynchronous environment, concurrent modifications to `obj` could lead to race conditions. For example, insecure code might not handle concurrency properly:
```javascript
// Insecure code without synchronization
obj[key] = {}; // Assumes single-threaded execution
```
```

deleted.line: target[key] = key in target ? target[key] : value;

added.line: target[key] = key in target ? target[key] : value;

commit_msg:
vuln-fix: Zip Slip Vulnerability

This fixes a Zip-Slip vulnerability.

This change does one of two things. This change either

1. Inserts a guard to protect against Zip Slip.
OR
2. Replaces `dir.getCanonicalPath().startsWith(parent.getCanonicalPath())`, which is vulnerable to partial path traversal attacks, with the more secure `dir.getCanonicalFile().toPath().startsWith(parent.getCanonicalFile().toPath())`.

For number 2, consider `"/usr/outnot".startsWith("/usr/out")`.
The check is bypassed although `/outnot` is not under the `/out` directory.
It's important to understand that the terminating slash may be removed when using various `String` representations of the `File` object.
For example, on Linux, `println(new File("/var"))` will print `/var`, but `println(new File("/var", "/")` will print `/var/`;
however, `println(new File("/var", "/").getCanonicalPath())` will print `/var`.

Weakness: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
Severity: High
CVSSS: 7.4
Detection: CodeQL (https://codeql.github.com/codeql-query-help/java/java-zipslip/) & OpenRewrite (https://public.moderne.io/recipes/org.openrewrite.java.security.ZipSlip)

Reported-by: Jonathan Leitschuh <Jonathan.Leitschuh@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Jonathan Leitschuh <Jonathan.Leitschuh@gmail.com>

Bug-tracker: https://github.com/JLLeitschuh/security-research/issues/16

Co-authored-by: Moderne <team@moderne.io>

[[1]]
[Code_Line]  if (!zipEntryPath.normalize().startsWith(jmeterDirectoryPath.normalize())) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-22
[Description] Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal'). If the JAR file contains malicious entries with paths like `../../evil.txt`, an attacker could exploit this to write files outside the intended directory. The check here mitigates this, but in insecure code, this check might be missing or improperly implemented.

[[2]]
[Code_Line]  Files.createDirectories(jmeterDirectoryPath.resolve(new File(jarFileEntry.getName()).getParentFile().getPath()));
[CWE_ID]CWE-400
[Description] Uncontrolled Resource Consumption ('Resource Exhaustion'). If the JAR file contains a large number of nested directories or maliciously crafted paths, this could lead to excessive resource consumption. In insecure code, there might be no limit or validation on the number of directories created.

[[3]]
[Code_Line]  Files.copy(configSettings.getInputStream(jarFileEntry),zipEntryPath);
[CWE_ID]CWE-502
[Description] Deserialization of Untrusted Data. If the JAR file is from an untrusted source, the `getInputStream` method could process malicious data. In insecure code, there might be no validation of the JAR file's source or contents, leading to potential exploitation.

[[4]]
[Code_Line]  throw new RuntimeException("Bad zip entry");
[CWE_ID]CWE-209
[Description] Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information. If this exception message is logged or exposed to an end user, it could reveal sensitive information about the file system structure or the application logic. In insecure code, such error messages might not be sanitized or properly handled.
```

commit_msg:
vuln-fix: Zip Slip Vulnerability



This fixes a Zip-Slip vulnerability.

This change does one of two things. This change either

1. Inserts a guard to protect against Zip Slip.
OR
2. Replaces `dir.getCanonicalPath().startsWith(parent.getCanonicalPath())`, which is vulnerable to partial path traversal attacks, with the more secure `dir.getCanonicalFile().toPath().startsWith(parent.getCanonicalFile().toPath())`.

For number 2, consider `"/usr/outnot".startsWith("/usr/out")`.
The check is bypassed although `/outnot` is not under the `/out` directory.
It's important to understand that the terminating slash may be removed when using various `String` representations of the `File` object.
For example, on Linux, `println(new File("/var"))` will print `/var`, but `println(new File("/var", "/")` will print `/var/`;
however, `println(new File("/var", "/").getCanonicalPath())` will print `/var`.

Weakness: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
Severity: High
CVSSS: 7.4
Detection: CodeQL (https://codeql.github.com/codeql-query-help/java/java-zipslip/) & OpenRewrite (https://public.moderne.io/recipes/org.openrewrite.java.security.ZipSlip)

[CWE_ID]CWE-22

If this check were missing or incorrect, an attacker could exploit a path traversal vulnerability by crafting a JAR entry name that escapes the intended directory, potentially overwriting critical files on the filesystem.
[[2]]

An insecure version might not handle large files properly, leading to denial of service.
[[3]]

[CWE_ID]CWE-404

commit_msg:
vuln-fix: Zip Slip Vulnerability

This fixes a Zip-Slip vulnerability.

This change does one of two things. This change either

1. Inserts a guard to protect against Zip Slip.
OR
2. Replaces `dir.getCanonicalPath().startsWith(parent.getCanonicalPath())`, which is vulnerable to partial path traversal attacks, with the more secure `dir.getCanonicalFile().toPath().startsWith(parent.getCanonicalFile().toPath())`.

For number 2, consider `"/usr/outnot".startsWith("/usr/out")`.
The check is bypassed although `/outnot` is not under the `/out` directory.
It's important to understand that the terminating slash may be removed when using various `String` representations of the `File` object.
For example, on Linux, `println(new File("/var"))` will print `/var`, but `println(new File("/var", "/")` will print `/var/`;
however, `println(new File("/var", "/").getCanonicalPath())` will print `/var`.

Weakness: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
Severity: High
CVSSS: 7.4
Detection: CodeQL (https://codeql.github.com/codeql-query-help/java/java-zipslip/) & OpenRewrite (https://public.moderne.io/recipes/org.openrewrite.java.security.ZipSlip)

Reported-by: Jonathan Leitschuh <Jonathan.Leitschuh@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Jonathan Leitschuh <Jonathan.Leitschuh@gmail.com>

Bug-tracker: https://github.com/JLLeitschuh/security-research/issues/16

Co-authored-by: Moderne <team@moderne.io>
description: Write a Java function to extract files from a ZIP archive to a specified directory with an option for safe extraction.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  if (!targetFile.toPath().normalize().startsWith(targetPath)) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-22
[Description]Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal'). If `targetFile` is not properly normalized and checked, an attacker could craft a ZIP file with entries that escape the target directory, leading to unauthorized file creation or overwriting. The critical line is the check itself, as it determines whether the path is safe.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  if (safeUnzip && isNotSafeFile(zipEntry.getName())) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. The function `isNotSafeFile` is used to determine if a file is safe, but if this function is not implemented correctly, it could allow unsafe files to be extracted. The critical line is the conditional check, as it determines whether the file is considered safe.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  os = new BufferedOutputStream(new FileOutputStream(targetFile));
[CWE_ID]CWE-362
[Description]Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition'). If the directory structure is modified between the check and the creation of the `FileOutputStream`, it could lead to a race condition where an attacker could manipulate the file system to write files outside the intended directory. The critical line is the creation of the `FileOutputStream`, as it is the point where the file is actually written.
[[4]]
[Code_Line]  close(is, os);
[CWE_ID]CWE-404
[Description]Improper Resource Shutdown or Release. If `close()` does not properly handle exceptions or if it fails to close the streams, it could lead to resource leaks. The critical line is the call to `close()`, as it is responsible for releasing the resources.
```

commit_msg:
# Absolute Path Traversal due to incorrect use of `send_file` call (#2025)

A path traversal attack (also known as directory traversal) aims to access files and directories that are stored outside the web root folder. By manipulating variables that reference files with “dot-dot-slash (../)” sequences and its variations or by using absolute file paths, it may be possible to access arbitrary files and directories stored on file system including application source code or configuration and critical system files. This attack is also known as “dot-dot-slash”, “directory traversal”, “directory climbing” and “backtracking”.

## Common Weakness Enumeration category
CWE - 36

## Root Cause Analysis

The `os.path.join` call is unsafe for use with untrusted input. When the `os.path.join` call encounters an absolute path, it ignores all the parameters it has encountered till that point and starts working with the new absolute path.  Please see the example below.
```
>>> import os.path
>>> static = "path/to/mySafeStaticDir"
>>> malicious = "/../../../../../etc/passwd"
>>> os.path.join(t,malicious)
'/../../../../../etc/passwd'
```
Since the "malicious" parameter represents an absolute path, the result of `os.path.join` ignores the static directory completely. Hence, untrusted input is passed via the `os.path.join` call to `flask.send_file` can lead to path traversal attacks.

In this case, the problems occurs due to the following code :
https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/0c0f9e33b36ee7ead0855f1464f8d4efad26bdbc/ganga/GangaGUI/gui/routes.py#L671

Here, the `path` parameter is attacker controlled. This parameter passes through the unsafe `os.path.join` call making the effective directory and filename passed to the `send_file` call attacker controlled. This leads to a path traversal attack.

## Proof of Concept

The bug can be verified using a proof of concept similar to the one shown below.

```
curl --path-as-is 'http://<domain>/job/<int:job_id>/browse///../../../../etc/passwd"'
```
## Remediation

This can be fixed by preventing flow of untrusted data to the vulnerable `send_file` function. In case the application logic necessiates this behaviour, one can either use the `werkzeug.utils.safe_join` to join untrusted paths or replace `flask.send_file` calls with `flask.send_from_directory` calls.

## Common Vulnerability Scoring System Vector

The attack can be carried over the network. A complex non-standard configuration or a specialized condition is not required for the attack to be successfully conducted. There is no user interaction required for successful execution. The attack can affect components outside the scope of the target module. The attack can be used to gain access to confidential files like passwords, login credentials and other secrets. It cannot be directly used to affect a change on a system resource. Hence has limited to no impact on integrity. Using this attack vector a attacker may make multiple requests for accessing huge files such as a database. This can lead to a partial system denial service. However, the impact on availability is quite low in this case. Taking this account an appropriate CVSS v3.1 vector would be

(AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:L)[https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln-metrics/cvss/v3-calculator?vector=AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:L&version=3.1]

This gives it a base score of 9.3/10 and a severity rating of critical.

## References
* [OWASP Path Traversal](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Path_Traversal)
* github/securitylab#669

### This bug was found using *[CodeQL by Github](https://codeql.github.com/)*

Co-authored-by: Porcupiney Hairs <porucpiney.hairs@protonmail.com>

output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  abs_path = safe_join(job_base_dir, path)
[CWE_ID]CWE-22
[Description]Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal'). If `safe_join` is not properly implemented, an attacker could manipulate the `path` variable to access directories outside the intended directory structure. An insecure version might directly concatenate paths without validation, allowing traversal attacks.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  job_info = query_internal_api(f"/internal/jobs/{job_id}", "get")
[CWE_ID]CWE-863
[Description]Incorrect Authorization. If `query_internal_api` does not enforce proper authorization checks, users might access job information they are not authorized to view. An insecure implementation might not verify the user's permissions against the job's access control policies.
[[3]]

[CWE_ID]CWE-552
[Description]Files or Directories Accessible to External Parties. If `abs_path` is not properly validated, sensitive files could be exposed. An insecure implementation might not check if the file is within an allowed directory, potentially exposing sensitive system files.
[[4]]

```

def archive_directory(top_dir, subdir, tmpdir):
    """
    .. function:: archive_directory(top_dir, subdir, tmpdir)

    Given a sub-directory name under the root directory to be archived, archive the contents of the sub-directory
    to a temporary directory. Then return the full path to the temporary directory.
    :param top_dir: The root path that will be archived and uploaded to Glacier.
    :param subdir: The path to the subdirectory that is being archived here, relative to `top_dir`
    :param tmpdir: The path to the temporary directory to store archives in until they are uploaded to Glacier
    :return: If the subdirectory contains files, then the full path to the temporary archives; otherwise, None
    """
    # We're only archiving the *files* in this directory, not the subdirectories.

    files = []
    full_backup_path = os.path.join(top_dir, subdir)
    dir_contents = os.listdir(full_backup_path)

    # Only add files to 'files' list, not subdirs
    for c in dir_contents:
        fpath = os.path.join(top_dir, subdir, c)
        if os.path.isfile(fpath) and not fpath.endswith(".ini"):
            # logger.info("Adding to archive list: {0}".format(c))
            files.append(fpath)

    if not files:
        # No point creating empty archives!
        return None

    os.makedirs(os.path.join(tmpdir, subdir))
    archive_file_path = os.path.join(tmpdir, subdir) + ".zip"

    logger.info("Archiving %s to %s" % (subdir, archive_file_path))

    # with open(os.devnull, "w") as devnull:

    devnull = open(os.devnull, "wb")
    file_list = []
    for p in os.listdir(full_backup_path):
        if os.path.isfile(os.path.join(full_backup_path, p)):
            file_list.append(os.path.join(full_backup_path, p))

    try:
        with zipfile.ZipFile(archive_file_path, "w", compression=zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED, allowZip64=True) as arch_zip:
            for f in file_list:
                logger.debug("Adding {0} to archive {1}".format(f, archive_file_path))
                arch_zip.write(f, os.path.basename(f))
                return archive_file_path

    except Exception, e:
        logging.error("Failed to create archive {0}: {1}".format(archive_file_path, e.message))
        logging.debug("Error args: {0}".format(e.args))
        return None

def check_max_open_files(opts):
    '''
    Check the number of max allowed open files and adjust if needed
    '''
    mof_c = opts.get('max_open_files', 100000)
    if sys.platform.startswith('win'):
        # Check the Windows API for more detail on this
        # http://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/xt874334(v=vs.71).aspx
        # and the python binding http://timgolden.me.uk/pywin32-docs/win32file.html
        mof_s = mof_h = win32file._getmaxstdio()
    else:
        mof_s, mof_h = resource.getrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_NOFILE)

    accepted_keys_dir = os.path.join(opts.get('pki_dir'), 'minions')
    accepted_count = len([
        key for key in os.listdir(accepted_keys_dir) if
        os.path.isfile(os.path.join(accepted_keys_dir, key))
    ])

    log.debug(
        'This salt-master instance has accepted {0} minion keys.'.format(
            accepted_count
        )
    )

    level = logging.INFO

    if (accepted_count * 4) <= mof_s:
        # We check for the soft value of max open files here because that's the
        # value the user chose to raise to.
        #
        # The number of accepted keys multiplied by four(4) is lower than the
        # soft value, everything should be OK
        return

    msg = (
        'The number of accepted minion keys({0}) should be lower than 1/4 '
        'of the max open files soft setting({1}). '.format(
            accepted_count, mof_s
        )
    )

    if accepted_count >= mof_s:
        # This should never occur, it might have already crashed
        msg += 'salt-master will crash pretty soon! '
        level = logging.CRITICAL
    elif (accepted_count * 2) >= mof_s:
        # This is way too low, CRITICAL
        level = logging.CRITICAL
    elif (accepted_count * 3) >= mof_s:
        level = logging.WARNING
        # The accepted count is more than 3 time, WARN
    elif (accepted_count * 4) >= mof_s:
        level = logging.INFO

    if mof_c < mof_h:
        msg += ('According to the system\'s hard limit, there\'s still a '
                'margin of {0} to raise the salt\'s max_open_files '
                'setting. ').format(mof_h - mof_c)

    msg += 'Please consider raising this value.'
    log.log(level=level, msg=msg)

commit_msg:
Do not waste CPU cycles on stat(2) on each _auth

Currently servers that handle many thousands of minions spend measurable time
doing only stat(2) calls.

In strace it looks like::

     0.000093 stat("/etc/localtime", {st_mode=S_IFREG|0644, st_size=118, ...}) = 0
     0.000236 open("/etc/salt/pki/master/minions", O_RDONLY|O_NONBLOCK|O_DIRECTORY|O_CLOEXEC) = 154
     0.011412 stat("/etc/salt/pki/master/minions/hostXXXX.linkedin.com", {st_mode=S_IFREG|0644, st_size=800, ...}) = 0
     0.000102 stat("/etc/salt/pki/master/minions/hostXXXX.linkedin.com", {st_mode=S_IFREG|0644, st_size=800, ...}) = 0
     ...many thousands lines...
     0.000064 stat("/etc/salt/pki/master/minions/hostXXXX.linkedin.com", {st_mode=S_IFREG|0644, st_size=800, ...}) = 0
     0.000062 stat("/etc/salt/pki/master/minions/hostXXXX.linkedin.com", {st_mode=S_IFREG|0644, st_size=796, ...}) = 0
     0.000485 stat("/export/apps/salt/log/master", {st_mode=S_IFREG|0644, st_size=37769598988, ...}) = 0
     0.000065 stat("/export/apps/salt/log/master", {st_mode=S_IFREG|0644, st_size=37769598988, ...}) = 0
     0.000184 stat("/etc/salt/pki/master/minions_rejected/hostXXXX.linkedin.com", 0x7fff28209f40) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or directory)
     0.000071 stat("/etc/salt/pki/master/minions/hostXXXX.linkedin.com", {st_mode=S_IFREG|0644, st_size=800, ...}) = 0
     0.000074 open("/etc/salt/pki/master/minions/hostXXXX.linkedin.com", O_RDONLY) = 154

Happens that on each _auth() call salt is counting files in pki/master/minions
and checks whenever those are regular files by applying os.path.isfile()
function to each which considerably slows things down.

This patch removes isfile() call that effectively makes check_max_open_files()
check less precise (but since it's already subject to external race conditions
like creation/removal of files by another process it should be OK) in exchange
for making it much faster.

PS. Note that calling salt.utils.verify.check_max_open_files() on each _auth is
not really efficient, it probably should be done periodically in a background
thread.

func_src_before:
def copy_over(source, dest):
    """
    Copies from the source to the destination, removing the destination
    if it exists and is a directory.
    """
    if os.path.exists(dest) and os.path.isdir(dest):
        shutil.rmtree(dest)
    shutil.copytree(source, dest)
    # mkdtemp will set the directory permissions to 700
    # for the webserver to read them, we need 755
    os.chmod(dest, stat.S_IRWXU | stat.S_IRGRP |
             stat.S_IXGRP | stat.S_IROTH | stat.S_IXOTH)
    shutil.rmtree(source)

def copy_over(source, dest):
    """
    Copies from the source to the destination, removing the destination
    if it exists and is a directory.
    """
    if os.path.exists(dest) and os.path.isdir(dest):
        shutil.rmtree(dest)
    shutil.copytree(force_bytes(source), force_bytes(dest))
    # mkdtemp will set the directory permissions to 700
    # for the webserver to read them, we need 755
    os.chmod(dest, stat.S_IRWXU | stat.S_IRGRP |
             stat.S_IXGRP | stat.S_IROTH | stat.S_IXOTH)
    shutil.rmtree(source)
commit_msg:
Fix unicode error on copying over extracted files from zip. (#3874)

Fix unicode error on copying over extracted files from zip.

Refs #3579

The problem here is that under very hard unit-testable/reproducable
circumstances a zip-file with unicode filenames get's extracted to a
temporary directory and while calling `copy_over` which calls
`shutil.copytree` which fails on our production systems with a
UnicodeDecodeError.

Reproducing this is something along the lines of

>>> shutil.copytree(u'/tmp/tmp8WG6A8/', u'/tmp/tmp8WG6A8-2/')
# doesn't error...
>>> shutil.copytree('/tmp/tmp8WG6A8/', u'/tmp/tmp8WG6A8-2/')
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode 	byte 0xd0 in ...

Checking all relevant environment configs in our production systems, all
LANG, LC_ALL and related configs are perfectly fine. It's not related to
uwsgi, it's not related to EFS so it's something else I have absolutely
no idea about :-/

commit_msg:
Remove autotest.tar.bz2 from image.zip

This change removes autotest.tar.bz2 from image.zip because archive_hwqual
no longer relies on this. Instead, it extracts the files directly from
autotest.tar.bz2 that is archived in the same directory as image.zip.

func_src_before:
def git_dir_info(path)
      ignored = @git_status.select { |file, mode| file.start_with?(path) && mode=='!!' }.keys
      present = Dir.deep_entries(path).map { |p| "#{path}/#{p}" }
      return '    ' if (present-ignored).empty?

      modes = (present-ignored).map { |file| @git_status[file] }-[nil]
      return '  ✓ '.colorize(@colors[:unchanged]) if modes.empty?
      Git.colored_status_symbols(modes.join.uniq, @colors)
    end
func_src_after:
def git_dir_info(path)
      direct_status = @git_status.fetch("#{path}/", nil)

      return Git.colored_status_symbols(direct_status.uniq, @colors) unless direct_status.nil?

      modes = @git_status.select { |file, mode| file.start_with?(path) && mode != '!!' }

      return '  ✓ '.colorize(@colors[:unchanged]) if modes.empty?

      Git.colored_status_symbols(modes.values.join.uniq, @colors)
    end

      return '' unless @git_status

      if content.directory?
        git_dir_info(relative_path)
      else

commit_msg: fix a buffer overflow
description: Write a C function to determine the current Git branch name and copy it into a wide character string buffer.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  strlcpy(pwd, getcwd(NULL, MAXPATHLEN), MAXPATHLEN);
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. The `getcwd` function can return NULL if it fails, which would lead to `strlcpy` receiving a NULL pointer, potentially causing undefined behavior. An insecure version might not check the return value of `getcwd`, leading to potential crashes or incorrect behavior.

[[2]]
[Code_Line]  snprintf(candidate, MAXPATHLEN, "%s/.git/HEAD", pwd);
[CWE_ID]CWE-120
[Description]Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow'). If `pwd` is close to `MAXPATHLEN` in length, the resulting string in `candidate` could exceed `MAXPATHLEN`, leading to a buffer overflow. An insecure version might not properly handle or check the length of `pwd` before using it in `snprintf`.

[[3]]
[Code_Line]  s = fread(buf, 1, size, fp);
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. The `size` parameter is used directly in `fread`, which could lead to reading more data than `buf` can hold if `size` exceeds `MAX_BRANCH_LEN`. An insecure version might not validate `size` against `MAX_BRANCH_LEN`, leading to buffer overflow.

[[4]]
[Code_Line]  buf[MAX_BRANCH_LEN - 1] = '\0';
[CWE_ID]CWE-170
[Description]Improper Null Termination. This line attempts to ensure null termination, but if `fread` reads `MAX_BRANCH_LEN` bytes, the null terminator will overwrite valid data. An insecure version might not correctly handle the null termination, leading to potential string handling issues.

[[5]]
[Code_Line]  fp = fopen(candidate, "r");
[CWE_ID]CWE-404
[Description]Improper Resource Shutdown or Release. The file pointer `fp` is opened but not checked for NULL before use, and while it is closed in some paths, an insecure version might not ensure `fclose` is called in all paths, leading to resource leaks.
```

description: Write a C function to cache the local IP address and port from a socket connection structure.

[CWE_ID]CWE-120
[Description]Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow'). The use of `strncpy` can lead to buffer overflow if `sock->ep_local` is not null-terminated within the first `sizeof(sock->ep_local2.host)` bytes. An insecure version might not ensure that the source string is properly null-terminated, leading to potential overflow.

The use of `snprintf` here assumes that `len` is correctly calculated and that there is enough space in `sock->ep_local` to append the port. While `inet_ntop` is generally safe, if `sock->ep_local` is not properly sized or if the address family is incorrect, it could lead to undefined behavior. An insecure version might not validate the address family or ensure the buffer is appropriately sized.

tmp_str array has a size of 1, so tmp_str[1] access is out of range.
Besides file_read_str() reads only one byte and strncmp() compares only
one byte too.

Signed-off-by: Yegor Yefremov <yegorslists@googlemail.com>

output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  sprintf(path, "/sys/class/pwm/pwmchip%d/pwm%d/polarity", pwm->chip, pwm->pwm);
[CWE_ID]CWE-120
[Description]Buffer Overflow. The use of `sprintf` can lead to buffer overflow if the formatted string exceeds the size of `path`. An insecure version might not check the length of the input values `pwm->chip` and `pwm->pwm`, potentially causing overflow if they are too large. Using `snprintf` with a specified buffer size is a safer alternative.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  char tmp_str[1];
[CWE_ID]CWE-131
[Description]Incorrect Calculation of Buffer Size. The buffer `tmp_str` is only 1 byte, which is insufficient for storing a string with a null terminator. An insecure version might not account for the null terminator, leading to buffer overflow or undefined behavior. Allocating a buffer of at least 2 bytes would be necessary to safely store a single character string.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  if (file_read_str(path, tmp_str, 1) == EXIT_FAILURE)
[CWE_ID]CWE-252
[Description]Unchecked Return Value. The return value of `file_read_str` is checked, but the function does not handle the specific error conditions that might arise from this call. An insecure version might ignore the specific error codes, leading to improper error handling. Proper error handling should include logging or specific actions based on different error codes.

[CWE_ID]CWE-476
[Description]NULL Pointer Dereference. The check for `pwm == NULL` is correctly implemented here, but an insecure version might omit this check, leading to dereferencing a NULL pointer and causing a segmentation fault or crash. Ensuring that pointers are validated before use is crucial to prevent such vulnerabilities.
```

```

commit_msg:
calibrate_cpuinfo: ensure we do not overflow buffer unit

cppcheck found a potential buffer overflow:

[common/os/os_util.c:212]: (error) Width 10 given in format string
  (no. 2) is larger than destination buffer 'unit[10]', use %9s to
  prevent overflowing it.

Increase unit array to 11 bytes.

Signed-off-by: Colin Ian King <colin.king@canonical.com>
description: Write a C function named `calibrate_cpuinfo` that reads the CPU frequency from a file and calculates the number of nanoseconds per clock cycle and the clock frequency in Hz.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  if ((f = fopen(CPUINFO_PATH, "r")) == NULL) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-362
[Description]Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition'). If `CPUINFO_PATH` is a file that can be modified by other processes, there is a potential race condition between checking the file's existence and reading its contents. An attacker could replace the file between these operations, leading to incorrect or malicious data being read.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  while (getline(&line, &len, f) > 0) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-404
[Description]Improper Resource Shutdown or Release. If `getline` fails or an error occurs before reaching `free(line)`, the allocated memory for `line` may not be released, leading to a memory leak. Proper error handling should ensure that `free(line)` is called in all cases.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  if (sscanf(line + strcspn(line, "@") + 1, "%lf%10s", &freq, unit) == 2) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. The code assumes that the line contains a valid frequency format after the '@' character. If the input file is malformed or maliciously crafted, this could lead to incorrect parsing and potentially undefined behavior. Input validation should ensure that the expected format is strictly adhered to.
[[4]]
[Code_Line]  debug_print(NULL, 2, "calibrate_cpuinfo: nsofclk = %.4f, " "clkofsec = %lu\n", *nsofclk, *clkofsec);
[CWE_ID]CWE-209
[Description]Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information. The debug message may expose sensitive information about the system's CPU frequency and timing details. If this information is logged or exposed to unauthorized users, it could be used to infer system capabilities or vulnerabilities.
```

commit_msg:
CBCC-1280: fix buffer overflow in address caching code

Change-Id: Ib5b3fd2bd252cf243d7c389fea1a0b3f1ed65411
Reviewed-on: http://review.couchbase.org/c/libcouchbase/+/157713
Tested-by: Build Bot <build@couchbase.com>
Reviewed-by: David Kelly <davidmichaelkelly@gmail.com>
description: In C, write a function to cache the local IP address and port from a socket connection structure.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  strncpy(sock->ep_local2.host, sock->ep_local, sizeof(sock->ep_local2.host));
[CWE_ID]CWE-120
[Description]Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow'). The use of `strncpy` can lead to buffer overflow if `sock->ep_local` is not null-terminated within the first `sizeof(sock->ep_local2.host)` characters. An insecure version might not ensure null-termination, leading to potential overflow.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  snprintf(sock->ep_local + len, sizeof(sock->ep_local) - len, ":%d", (int)ntohs(addr->sin_port));
[CWE_ID]CWE-120
[Description]Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow'). The `snprintf` function is used here to append the port to `sock->ep_local`. If `len` is close to `sizeof(sock->ep_local)`, this could lead to buffer overflow. An insecure version might not correctly calculate the remaining buffer size, leading to overflow.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  inet_ntop(AF_INET, &(addr->sin_addr), sock->ep_local, sizeof(sock->ep_local));
[CWE_ID]CWE-676
[Description]Use of Potentially Dangerous Function. The `inet_ntop` function is generally safe, but if `sock->ep_local` is not properly sized or if the address family is incorrect, it could lead to undefined behavior. An insecure version might not validate the address family or buffer size before calling `inet_ntop`.
```

commit_msg: A one-byte buffer overflow has been fixed.
description: In C, write a function to validate and log the remote host's RFB protocol version, then respond with the server's version.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  remote_major = atoi(&buf[4]);
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. The use of `atoi` can lead to undefined behavior if the input is not a valid integer representation. If `buf` contains unexpected characters, `atoi` may not handle them properly, potentially leading to incorrect parsing or security issues. A more secure approach would be to use `strtol` with proper error checking.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  sprintf(ver_msg, "RFB %03d.%03d\n", abs(major) % 999, abs(minor) % 999);
[CWE_ID]CWE-120
[Description]Buffer Overflow. The use of `sprintf` can lead to buffer overflow if the formatted data exceeds the buffer size. Although the current format seems safe, using `snprintf` would be a more secure practice as it limits the number of characters written to the buffer, preventing overflow.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  aio_write(NULL, ver_msg, 12);
[CWE_ID]CWE-404
[Description]Improper Resource Shutdown or Release. If `aio_write` fails, there is no error handling to ensure that resources are properly released or that the system is left in a consistent state. Proper error handling should be implemented to manage such failures.
[[4]]
[Code_Line]  aio_setread(rf_host_auth, NULL, 4);
[CWE_ID]CWE-362
[Description]Race Condition. The function `aio_setread` sets up an asynchronous read operation, which could lead to race conditions if shared resources are accessed without proper synchronization. Ensure that shared resources are protected with appropriate locking mechanisms.
```

commit_msg: Buffer Overflow fixed (https://github.com/picoflamingo/BBCape_EEPROM/issues/1)
description: Write a C function named `set_eeprom_serial_number` that copies a serial number string into an EEPROM structure and sets a dirty flag.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  strncpy (e->serial, sn, 12);
[CWE_ID]CWE-120
[Description]Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow'). The use of `strncpy` without ensuring that `sn` is null-terminated or that its length is less than or equal to 11 characters (to leave space for the null terminator) can lead to buffer overflow. An insecure version might not check the length of `sn`, potentially causing overflow if `sn` is longer than expected.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  strncpy (e->serial, sn, 12);
[CWE_ID]CWE-787
[Description]Out-of-bounds Write. If `sn` is not properly null-terminated and longer than 12 characters, `strncpy` will not append a null terminator to `e->serial`, potentially causing out-of-bounds writes when `e->serial` is used later. An insecure version might not ensure that `e->serial` is null-terminated after copying.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  char *sn
[CWE_ID]CWE-787
[Description]Out-of-bounds Write. If `sn` is not properly validated before being passed to this function, it could lead to out-of-bounds writes if `sn` is longer than expected. An insecure version might not validate the length of `sn` before using it.
```

commit_msg:
Fix buffer overflow in dmi_memory_device_size_str()
Close #63
description: Write a C function named `dmi_memory_device_size_str` that takes a 16-bit unsigned integer and returns a string representation of memory size.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  static char size[16];
[CWE_ID]CWE-121
[Description]Stack-based Buffer Overflow. The `size` buffer is statically allocated with a fixed size of 16 bytes. If the `sprintf` function writes more than 16 bytes into `size`, it can cause a buffer overflow. This can happen if the `code` value is large enough to produce a string longer than 15 characters (plus the null terminator). An insecure version might not check the length of the formatted string, leading to potential overflow.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  sprintf(size, "%u kB", code & 0x7FFF);
[CWE_ID]CWE-134
[Description]Uncontrolled Format String. The use of `sprintf` with a format string that includes user-controlled data (in this case, `code`) can lead to format string vulnerabilities if not properly handled. Although the current code uses a fixed format, an insecure version might allow user input to directly influence the format string, leading to potential security issues.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  return size;
[CWE_ID]CWE-562
[Description]Return of Stack Variable Address. The function returns a pointer to a statically allocated buffer on the stack. If this function is called multiple times, the returned pointer will point to the same memory location, potentially leading to unexpected behavior if the buffer is modified. An insecure version might return a pointer to a local variable, which becomes invalid once the function exits.
```

			.antMatchers("/").permitAll()

			.antMatchers("/**/*.{js,html,css,json}").access("hasRole('ROLE_ADMIN') or hasRole('ROLE_PBUSER')")
	        .antMatchers("/**/app/**").access("hasRole('ROLE_ADMIN') or hasRole('ROLE_PBUSER')")

	        .oauth2Login()
	        	.successHandler(myAuthenticationSuccessHandler())
	        		.userInfoEntspint()
	        	.userService(injectLocalRolesOAuth2UserService())
	        .and().and()

	        .deleteCookies("JSESSIONID")
	        .logoutSuccessHandler(oidcLogoutSuccessHandler());

commit_msg:
HYD-6493 Improve Crypto Based on TLS config Doc

Our crypto is currently weak (and weaker still in past releases).
We are currently allowing DH+3DES which should be blacklisted.
We have already disabled SSL variants, but we should also disable older
TLS as it seems we can support it.

Intel has a doc on crypto (attached to ticket) with a ranking of
preferred ciphers. We are able
to support the second cipher in the list without issue,
TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256.

By enabling only TLS 1.2 and TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 we
are in good
standing with Intel's stance on crypto

  - Update Nginx config to only support TLS 1.2.
  - Use TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 as the 
    only allowed cipher.

Change-Id: I98c486b9c5af4b4ba34163623b1a1e59655ffa3c
Signed-off-by: Joe Grund <joe.grund@intel.com>

Using `ssl.PROTOCOL_SSLv3` in `ssl.wrap_socket` can lead to insecure connections if the server does not properly reject SSLv3. func_src_before:
def _connect(self):
        """Connect to the host and port specified in __init__."""
        if self.sock:
            return
        if self._proxy_host is not None:
            logger.info('Connecting to http proxy %s:%s',
                        self._proxy_host, self._proxy_port)
            sock = socketutil.create_connection((self._proxy_host,
                                                 self._proxy_port))
            if self.ssl:
                # TODO proxy header support
                data = self._buildheaders('CONNECT', '%s:%d' % (self.host,
                                                                self.port),
                                          {}, HTTP_VER_1_0)
                sock.send(data)
                sock.setblocking(0)
                r = self.response_class(sock, self.timeout, 'CONNECT')
                timeout_exc = HTTPTimeoutException(
                    'Timed out waiting for CONNECT response from proxy')
                while not r.complete():
                    try:
                        # We're a friend of the response class, so let
                        # us use the private attribute.
                        # pylint: disable=W0212
                        if not r._select():
                            if not r.complete():
                                raise timeout_exc
                    except HTTPTimeoutException:
                        # This raise/except pattern looks goofy, but
                        # _select can raise the timeout as well as the
                        # loop body. I wish it wasn't this convoluted,
                        # but I don't have a better solution
                        # immediately handy.
                        raise timeout_exc
                if r.status != 200:
                    raise HTTPProxyConnectFailedException(
                        'Proxy connection failed: %d %s' % (r.status,
                                                            r.read()))
                logger.info('CONNECT (for SSL) to %s:%s via proxy succeeded.',
                            self.host, self.port)
        else:
            sock = socketutil.create_connection((self.host, self.port))
        if self.ssl:
            # This is the default, but in the case of proxied SSL
            # requests the proxy logic above will have cleared
            # blocking mode, so re-enable it just to be safe.
            sock.setblocking(1)
            logger.debug('wrapping socket for ssl with options %r',
                         self.ssl_opts)
            sock = socketutil.wrap_socket(sock, **self.ssl_opts)
            if self._ssl_validator:
                self._ssl_validator(sock)
        sock.setblocking(0)
        self.sock = sock

commit_msg:
httpclient: import 4bb625347d4a to provide SSL wrapper injection

This lets us inject our own ssl.wrap_socket equivalent into
httpclient, which means that any changes we make to our ssl handling
can be *entirely* on our side without having to muck with httpclient,
which sounds appealing. For example, an extension could wrap
sslutil.ssl_wrap_socket with an api-compatible wrapper and then tweak
SSL settings more precisely or use GnuTLS instead of OpenSSL.

commit_msg:
Tests: Stop using deprecated `ssl.wrap_socket`

Use `context.wrap_socket` instead. On this context, use a minimum
version to restrict to secure TLS protocol variants only.

This was reported as a check failure by CodeQL code scanning with id
`py/insecure-default-protocol`.

https://github.com/github/codeql/blob/main/python/ql/src/Security/CWE-327/InsecureDefaultProtocol.qhelp

[CWE_ID]CWE-295
[Description]Improper Certificate Validation. Using `_create_unverified_context` disables certificate verification, which can lead to man-in-the-middle attacks. An insecure version of this code would use `ssl._create_unverified_context` without any verification, allowing attackers to intercept and manipulate data.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  cert_reqs=ssl.CERT_OPTIONAL,
[CWE_ID]CWE-295
[Description]Improper Certificate Validation. Setting `cert_reqs` to `ssl.CERT_OPTIONAL` means that the server will not require a client certificate, which can lead to weak authentication. An insecure version of this code would not enforce certificate validation, allowing unauthorized clients to connect.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  check_hostname=False,
[CWE_ID]CWE-297
[Description]Improper Validation of Certificate with Host Mismatch. Disabling hostname checking (`check_hostname=False`) can allow attackers to use a valid certificate for a different hostname, leading to man-in-the-middle attacks. An insecure version of this code would not verify that the certificate matches the hostname, allowing for potential spoofing.
```
