commit_msg:
Fix integer overflow in STRALGO LCS (CVE-2021-29477)

An integer overflow bug in Redis version 6.0 or newer could be exploited using
the STRALGO LCS command to corrupt the heap and potentially result with remote
code execution.
description: Write a C function named `stralgoLCS` that computes the longest common subsequence (LCS) of two strings.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  uint32_t *lcs = zmalloc((size_t)(alen+1)*(blen+1)*sizeof(uint32_t));
[CWE_ID]CWE-190
[Description]Integer Overflow or Wraparound. The multiplication `(alen+1)*(blen+1)*sizeof(uint32_t)` could potentially overflow if `alen` and `blen` are large enough, leading to insufficient memory allocation and buffer overflow. In insecure code, this could be exploited by providing very large input strings, causing the multiplication to wrap around and allocate less memory than needed, leading to memory corruption.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  if (getLongLongFromObjectOrReply(c,c->argv[j+1],&minmatchlen,NULL) != C_OK) goto cleanup;
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. The function `getLongLongFromObjectOrReply` is used to parse a long long integer from user input. If this function does not properly validate the input, it could lead to unexpected behavior or vulnerabilities such as integer overflow or injection attacks. In insecure code, failing to validate input could allow an attacker to pass malicious data that could cause the application to behave incorrectly or crash.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  obja = lookupKeyRead(c->db,c->argv[j+1]);
[CWE_ID]CWE-862
[Description]Missing Authorization. The function `lookupKeyRead` retrieves a key from the database without checking if the user has the necessary permissions to access it. In insecure code, this could allow unauthorized users to access or manipulate data they should not have access to, leading to potential data breaches or privilege escalation.
[[4]]
[Code_Line]  if ((obja && obja->type != OBJ_STRING) || (objb && objb->type != OBJ_STRING))
[CWE_ID]CWE-704
[Description]Incorrect Type Handling. This line checks if the objects are of type `OBJ_STRING`. If the type checking is not robust, it could lead to type confusion vulnerabilities where an attacker could manipulate the type of the object to execute unintended operations. In insecure code, improper type handling could allow attackers to exploit type mismatches to cause crashes or execute arbitrary code.
[[5]]
[Code_Line]  if (a == NULL) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-754
```

commit_msg:
Fix potential integer overflow loading ELF files (#148)

When loading ELF files we calculate the location of a section header in
a way that can overflow a `uint32_t` value producing a wrong result.

This commit fixes the issue by using the fact that section headers are
contiguous in the ELF file.

Signed-off-by: Matthew Gretton-Dann <matthew.gretton-dann@arm.com>

Signed-off-by: Matthew Gretton-Dann <matthew.gretton-dann@arm.com>
description: Write a C function named `ubpf_load_elf` that loads an ELF file into a uBPF virtual machine and handles errors.
output:
```
[[1]]
[Code_Line]  const Elf64_Ehdr *ehdr = bounds_check(&b, 0, sizeof(*ehdr));
[CWE_ID]CWE-125
[Description]Out-of-bounds Read. If `elf` is not properly validated before being passed to this function, `bounds_check` might return a pointer to an invalid memory location, leading to an out-of-bounds read. Insecure code might not perform sufficient checks on `elf` and `elf_size` before accessing memory.
[[2]]
[Code_Line]  text_copy = malloc(text->size);
[CWE_ID]CWE-401
[Description]Memory Leak. If an error occurs after this allocation and before `text_copy` is freed, it could lead to a memory leak. Insecure code might not have proper error handling to ensure `free` is called in all error paths.
[[3]]
[Code_Line]  if (memcmp(ehdr->e_ident, ELFMAG, SELFMAG)) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-20
[Description]Improper Input Validation. The function assumes that `ehdr->e_ident` is at least `SELFMAG` bytes long, which might not be true if `elf` is malformed. Insecure code might not validate the length of `e_ident` before using it.
[[4]]
[Code_Line]  unsigned int imm = ubpf_lookup_registered_function(vm, sym_name);
[CWE_ID]CWE-704
[Description]Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast. The function `ubpf_lookup_registered_function` returns an `unsigned int`, but `-1` is used to check for errors, which can lead to incorrect error handling due to type conversion issues. Insecure code might not handle the return value correctly, leading to logic errors.
[[5]]
[Code_Line]  if (r->r_offset + 8 > text->size) {
[CWE_ID]CWE-190
[Description]Integer Overflow or Wraparound. If `r->r_offset` is close to the maximum value of an integer, adding 8 could cause an overflow, leading to incorrect bounds checking. Insecure code might not check for potential overflow conditions before performing arithmetic operations.
```

commit_msg:
Change comparison operator from == to >= in size checks.

Given that the right hand value is the maximum int value, this is functionally equivalent.

However, using this operator aligns with the CodeQL expectation that the guard expression insures that the value is "less than, or equal to, the maximum value of the type".

References:
https://github.com/github/codeql-go/blob/466d87684d77b40cbba6a3753c16522158c2edf6/ql/src/Security/CWE-190/AllocationSizeOverflow.qhelp#L26-L27

https://github.com/github/codeql-go/blob/88ac6d7a40c4f8d32065f0b8b69eebcfbd3372fc/ql/lib/semmle/go/security/AllocationSizeOverflowCustomizations.qll#L78

def escape_data(note)
  note = note.sub("'", "\\\\'")
  note = note.sub('"', '\"')
  note = note.sub(" ", "\\ ")
  note = note.sub("\\", "\\\\\\\\\\")

  return note
end
func_src_after:
def escape_data(note)
  note = note.gsub("'", "\\\\'")
  note = note.gsub('"', '\"')
  note = note.gsub(" ", "\\ ")

  return note
end

  end
